content.content:
=== #476 ===
[OPEN] profiler: identifier-casing & CSV-delimiter limitations in column introspection/profiling
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

Surfaced during review of #475 (column-dropdown). These are **pre-existing limitations of the profiler** that the new `list_columns` introspection path inherits/newly surfaces — tracking separately rather than blocking #475.

## 1. Identifier casing (Snowflake / Unity Catalog)
`build_columns_query` issues an unquoted `SELECT * … LIMIT 0`, so `result.keys()` returns the **warehouse-cased** names (Snowflake folds unquoted identifiers to UPPERCASE → `AMOUNT`, not `amount`). The dropdown then offers `AMOUNT`. This is arguably *correct* (those are the real stored names, and the profiler/aggregate path also emits unquoted names that fold the same way), but:
- a **quoted mixed-case** column (`"Amount"`) is stored as `Amount`; the dropdown surfaces `Amount`, but the unquoted profiler/aggregate path folds it to `AMOUNT` and cannot find it. `validate_identifier` also rejects names needing quoting.
- The combobox (#475) lets the author free-type, so this is not a hard block — but quoted/mixed-case identifiers are unsupported end-to-end in the profiler.

**Fix direction:** decide whether v1 supports quoted/mixed-case identifiers; if so, thread a quoting strategy through `validate_identifier` + the Core builders (`_table`, aggregate, top-values, columns) consistently.

## 2. CSV delimiter assumption (flat files)
`pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=0)` (column listing) and `_read_dataframe` (profiling) both use pandas defaults → **comma delimiter**. A `;`/tab-delimited CSV yields one bogus "column" (the whole header line) with no error — a silent wrong answer that defeats the dropdown for that file. Shared by both the profiler and the new lister.

**Fix direction:** sniff the delimiter (`csv.Sniffer` on the header) or accept a delimiter hint on the flat-file connection/target config; apply it consistently in `list_file_columns` and `_read_dataframe`.

Both are low-severity (combobox free-text mitigates #1; #2 matches existing profiler behavior). Post-v1.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Profiler handles case-sensitive/quoted identifiers correctly on all SQL paths
- [ ] CSV delimiter is configurable (or inferred) end-to-end in introspection + profiling
- [ ] Regression tests for both limitations

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #520 ===
[OPEN] Freshness/volume monitors don't support flat-file (ADLS/S3) datasources — SQL-only
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

## Problem
The freshness & volume monitor kinds (ADR 0012, #426/#437) are evaluated via `run_monitors`, which is implemented **only** on the SQL runners:

- `backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py` — `SnowflakeCheckRunner.run_monitors` (scalar SQL aggregate: `SELECT MAX(<col>)` / `COUNT(*)`)
- `backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py` — `UnityCatalogCheckRunner.run_monitors`

`FlatFileCheckRunner` (`backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py`) has **no** `run_monitors`. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` builds the metric as a SQL string (`build_monitor_sql` → `SELECT MAX(column) FROM {target}`), which has no meaning for a flat file read into a DataFrame.

## Impact
- You **cannot** author a freshness (or volume) monitor against an ADLS Gen2 / S3 flat-file connection — the check editor only offers Freshness/Volume for Snowflake/UC.
- Concretely: the demo/test harness (ADR 0021) seeds a deliberate **flat-file freshness** issue — `inventory_snapshot.load_ts` on the latest ADLS Parquet pushed +26h back — and DataQ has **no way to catch it**, because inventory is a flat-file-in-place (Flow C), not a warehouse table. Freshness on the *source* landing zone is arguably the most valuable place to monitor it (it's where "the source stopped producing" first shows up), and it's exactly the gap.
- Discovered while validating live freshness alerting end-to-end (freshness on Snowflake `ORDERS_HEADER.ORDER_TS` works + alerts to Slack; the flat-file equivalent is impossible).

## Direction (not prescriptive)
Give `FlatFileCheckRunner` a `run_monitors` that computes the same metrics without SQL:
- **freshness** — either (a) `MAX(<timestamp column>)` over the resolved batch DataFrame (read the column, take the max), or (b) the **file/blob `last_modified`** of the resolved batch (a "when did this file land" freshness, no column needed). (a) mirrors the SQL semantics; (b) is cheaper and catches "no new file" even when the in-file timestamps are stale/absent. Possibly offer both.
- **volume** — `len(df)` (row count) of the resolved batch — trivial once the batch is read.

The monitor-kind seam (`check.kind`, numeric `metric_value`, ADR 0012) already accommodates this; the change is a runner-level `run_monitors` impl + letting the check editor expose Freshness/Volume for flat-file connections. `monitors.py` would need a non-SQL evaluation path (the age math in `_freshness_age_hours` / `_as_aware_datetime` is already datasource-agnostic and reusable).

## Acceptance
- A freshness monitor can be authored + run against an ADLS/S3 flat-file connection and correctly flags a stale latest batch.
- A volume monitor likewise flags a thinned/short batch.
- The harness's seeded flat-file freshness + volume-drop issues on `inventory_snapshot` are caught.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `freshness` + `volume` monitors run on flat-file connections (S3 + local; ADLS code path covered by tests)
- [ ] Semantics documented (e.g. newest-file mtime for freshness; row/file counts for volume over the matched batch)
- [ ] UI no longer gates monitor kinds to SQL datasources; tests cover the new paths

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #124 ===
[OPEN] Add DQ-dimension classification to checks (Completeness/Uniqueness/Validity/…)
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement,priority/P2

## Context
Checks currently have **two** classification axes, neither of which is a data-quality *dimension*:

| Field | Axis | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `kind` | *how* the monitor works (ADR 0012) | `expectation` / `freshness` / `comparison` |
| `expectation_type` | the specific GX rule | `expect_column_values_to_be_unique` |

A **DQ dimension** (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness) is a third axis — the *semantic category* of what quality aspect a check measures. It powers dimension-rollup dashboards ("98% complete, 99.5% unique, 95% valid…"), which are a standard, valued DQ-platform feature and would feed the Week-6 health views.

We don't have it today (confirmed: no `dimension` field in the model, roadmap, or ADRs).

## Why deferred (not front-loaded)
Unlike the severity-tier `status` and threshold columns (ADR 0005) or `check.kind`/`metric_value` (ADR 0012) — which had to ride the one-shot Week-3 migration because retrofitting them is an expensive *narrowing*/data-rewrite — a `dimension` column is a **cheap additive nullable column addable anytime** (pure widening, no two-step). So there's no schema-timing pressure; it deserves a deliberate design pass rather than a rushed column in PR-B2.

## Decisions to make first (ADR-worthy)
1. **Taxonomy** — adopt the canonical 6 dimensions? (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness.) Enum/CHECK vs free-form?
2. **Stored vs auto-derived** — many GX expectations map deterministically to a dimension (`expect_column_values_to_be_unique` → Uniqueness; `…_to_not_be_null` → Completeness; `…_to_be_between`/`…_to_match_regex` → Validity). Do we **store** `dimension` on the check (user-set, override-able) or **derive** it from `expectation_type` via a lookup? Or store with a derived default?
3. **Interaction with `kind`** — freshness/`comparison` monitors map to Timeliness/Consistency respectively; make sure the mapping spans all kinds, not just GX expectations.
4. **Surface** — check editor dropdown (Week 4), dashboard dimension filter/rollup (Week 6).

## Implementation sketch (once decided)
- Additive migration: nullable `checks.dimension` (+ CHECK if enum). No data rewrite, no two-step.

=== #476 ===
[OPEN] profiler: identifier-casing & CSV-delimiter limitations in column introspection/profiling
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

Surfaced during review of #475 (column-dropdown). These are **pre-existing limitations of the profiler** that the new `list_columns` introspection path inherits/newly surfaces — tracking separately rather than blocking #475.

## 1. Identifier casing (Snowflake / Unity Catalog)
`build_columns_query` issues an unquoted `SELECT * … LIMIT 0`, so `result.keys()` returns the **warehouse-cased** names (Snowflake folds unquoted identifiers to UPPERCASE → `AMOUNT`, not `amount`). The dropdown then offers `AMOUNT`. This is arguably *correct* (those are the real stored names, and the profiler/aggregate path also emits unquoted names that fold the same way), but:
- a **quoted mixed-case** column (`"Amount"`) is stored as `Amount`; the dropdown surfaces `Amount`, but the unquoted profiler/aggregate path folds it to `AMOUNT` and cannot find it. `validate_identifier` also rejects names needing quoting.
- The combobox (#475) lets the author free-type, so this is not a hard block — but quoted/mixed-case identifiers are unsupported end-to-end in the profiler.

**Fix direction:** decide whether v1 supports quoted/mixed-case identifiers; if so, thread a quoting strategy through `validate_identifier` + the Core builders (`_table`, aggregate, top-values, columns) consistently.

## 2. CSV delimiter assumption (flat files)
`pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=0)` (column listing) and `_read_dataframe` (profiling) both use pandas defaults → **comma delimiter**. A `;`/tab-delimited CSV yields one bogus "column" (the whole header line) with no error — a silent wrong answer that defeats the dropdown for that file. Shared by both the profiler and the new lister.

**Fix direction:** sniff the delimiter (`csv.Sniffer` on the header) or accept a delimiter hint on the flat-file connection/target config; apply it consistently in `list_file_columns` and `_read_dataframe`.

Both are low-severity (combobox free-text mitigates #1; #2 matches existing profiler behavior). Post-v1.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Profiler handles case-sensitive/quoted identifiers correctly on all SQL paths
- [ ] CSV delimiter is configurable (or inferred) end-to-end in introspection + profiling
- [ ] Regression tests for both limitations

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #520 ===
[OPEN] Freshness/volume monitors don't support flat-file (ADLS/S3) datasources — SQL-only
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

## Problem
The freshness & volume monitor kinds (ADR 0012, #426/#437) are evaluated via `run_monitors`, which is implemented **only** on the SQL runners:

- `backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py` — `SnowflakeCheckRunner.run_monitors` (scalar SQL aggregate: `SELECT MAX(<col>)` / `COUNT(*)`)
- `backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py` — `UnityCatalogCheckRunner.run_monitors`

`FlatFileCheckRunner` (`backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py`) has **no** `run_monitors`. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` builds the metric as a SQL string (`build_monitor_sql` → `SELECT MAX(column) FROM {target}`), which has no meaning for a flat file read into a DataFrame.

## Impact
- You **cannot** author a freshness (or volume) monitor against an ADLS Gen2 / S3 flat-file connection — the check editor only offers Freshness/Volume for Snowflake/UC.
- Concretely: the demo/test harness (ADR 0021) seeds a deliberate **flat-file freshness** issue — `inventory_snapshot.load_ts` on the latest ADLS Parquet pushed +26h back — and DataQ has **no way to catch it**, because inventory is a flat-file-in-place (Flow C), not a warehouse table. Freshness on the *source* landing zone is arguably the most valuable place to monitor it (it's where "the source stopped producing" first shows up), and it's exactly the gap.
- Discovered while validating live freshness alerting end-to-end (freshness on Snowflake `ORDERS_HEADER.ORDER_TS` works + alerts to Slack; the flat-file equivalent is impossible).

## Direction (not prescriptive)
Give `FlatFileCheckRunner` a `run_monitors` that computes the same metrics without SQL:
- **freshness** — either (a) `MAX(<timestamp column>)` over the resolved batch DataFrame (read the column, take the max), or (b) the **file/blob `last_modified`** of the resolved batch (a "when did this file land" freshness, no column needed). (a) mirrors the SQL semantics; (b) is cheaper and catches "no new file" even when the in-file timestamps are stale/absent. Possibly offer both.
- **volume** — `len(df)` (row count) of the resolved batch — trivial once the batch is read.

The monitor-kind seam (`check.kind`, numeric `metric_value`, ADR 0012) already accommodates this; the change is a runner-level `run_monitors` impl + letting the check editor expose Freshness/Volume for flat-file connections. `monitors.py` would need a non-SQL evaluation path (the age math in `_freshness_age_hours` / `_as_aware_datetime` is already datasource-agnostic and reusable).

## Acceptance
- A freshness monitor can be authored + run against an ADLS/S3 flat-file connection and correctly flags a stale latest batch.
- A volume monitor likewise flags a thinned/short batch.
- The harness's seeded flat-file freshness + volume-drop issues on `inventory_snapshot` are caught.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `freshness` + `volume` monitors run on flat-file connections (S3 + local; ADLS code path covered by tests)
- [ ] Semantics documented (e.g. newest-file mtime for freshness; row/file counts for volume over the matched batch)
- [ ] UI no longer gates monitor kinds to SQL datasources; tests cover the new paths

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #124 ===
[OPEN] Add DQ-dimension classification to checks (Completeness/Uniqueness/Validity/…)
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement,priority/P2

## Context
Checks currently have **two** classification axes, neither of which is a data-quality *dimension*:

| Field | Axis | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `kind` | *how* the monitor works (ADR 0012) | `expectation` / `freshness` / `comparison` |
| `expectation_type` | the specific GX rule | `expect_column_values_to_be_unique` |

A **DQ dimension** (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness) is a third axis — the *semantic category* of what quality aspect a check measures. It powers dimension-rollup dashboards ("98% complete, 99.5% unique, 95% valid…"), which are a standard, valued DQ-platform feature and would feed the Week-6 health views.

We don't have it today (confirmed: no `dimension` field in the model, roadmap, or ADRs).

## Why deferred (not front-loaded)
Unlike the severity-tier `status` and threshold columns (ADR 0005) or `check.kind`/`metric_value` (ADR 0012) — which had to ride the one-shot Week-3 migration because retrofitting them is an expensive *narrowing*/data-rewrite — a `dimension` column is a **cheap additive nullable column addable anytime** (pure widening, no two-step). So there's no schema-timing pressure; it deserves a deliberate design pass rather than a rushed column in PR-B2.

## Decisions to make first (ADR-worthy)
1. **Taxonomy** — adopt the canonical 6 dimensions? (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness.) Enum/CHECK vs free-form?
2. **Stored vs auto-derived** — many GX expectations map deterministically to a dimension (`expect_column_values_to_be_unique` → Uniqueness; `…_to_not_be_null` → Completeness; `…_to_be_between`/`…_to_match_regex` → Validity). Do we **store** `dimension` on the check (user-set, override-able) or **derive** it from `expectation_type` via a lookup? Or store with a derived default?
3. **Interaction with `kind`** — freshness/`comparison` monitors map to Timeliness/Consistency respectively; make sure the mapping spans all kinds, not just GX expectations.
4. **Surface** — check editor dropdown (Week 4), dashboard dimension filter/rollup (Week 6).

## Implementation sketch (once decided)
- Additive migration: nullable `checks.dimension` (+ CHECK if enum). No data rewrite, no two-step.
- Add to check CRUD request/response schemas + a `expectation_type → dimension` lookup if auto-deriving.
- Dashboard: `GROUP BY dimension` rollup alongside the ADR-0005 health score.

## When
Post-B2 / alongside the Week-6 dashboard, or whenever dimension reporting becomes a concrete requirement. Decision recorded via PR #123 follow-up (kept out of PR-B2 deliberately).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Every check carries a DQ-dimension classification (auto-derived from expectation/monitor type, user-overridable)
- [ ] Dimension visible in the check editor and results surfaces; export/import round-trips it
- [ ] Backward-compatible migration (existing checks get a derived default)

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

content.text:
Recon complete. Findings below (all paths absolute).

## 1. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` (438 lines)

**Structure:** pure, DB-free core + a per-kind strategy registry (#726).

- **`build_monitor_sql(kind, *, table, schema, catalog, config) -> str`** — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/monitors.py:95-110`. Looks up `_strategy(kind)`; if `strategy.build_sql is None` raises `MonitorConfigError(f"monitor kind {kind!r} has no scalar-SQL form")` (:108). Builds the target via `qualified_table` (:74-92, which rejects catalog-without-schema at :82-86), then delegates to the strategy's `build_sql`. Concrete builders: `_freshness_sql` at :202-204 → `f"SELECT MAX({column}) FROM {target}"`; `_volume_sql` at :239-240 → `f"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {target}"`. Identifier safety comes only from `_ident` (:62-71) → `is_sql_identifier`.
- **`_freshness_age_hours(max_timestamp, now) -> float`** — :113-116. `max((now - max_timestamp).total_seconds() / 3600.0, 0.0)` — clamps clock-skew futures to 0.
- **`_as_aware_datetime(scalar, column) -> datetime`** — :119-134. Accepts `datetime`, else `date` → `datetime.combine(scalar, time.min)` (:129, note `datetime` checked first since it subclasses `date`), else raises `MonitorConfigError`. Naive → `.replace(tzinfo=UTC)` (:134).
- **Registry:** `MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY` :317-327 with `MonitorKindStrategy` dataclass (:302-314: `kind`, `validate_config`, `outcome`, `build_sql | None`). Derived tuples at :337-342: `MONITOR_KINDS`, `SCALAR_MONITOR_KINDS` (`build_sql is not None` → freshness, volume), `STATEFUL_MONITOR_KINDS` (→ schema_drift). Registration is import-time only (comment :331-336).
- **Shared execution helpers:** `run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, *, monitors, now) -> list[CheckOutcome]` :352-385 (datasource-agnostic banding loop, per-monitor error isolation at :376-384); `evaluate_monitors(fetch_scalar, ...)` :388-409; `run_monitors_over_engine(engine, *, table, schema, catalog, monitors)` :412-438 (opens ONE connection, `conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar()`).

**`run_monitors` interface** — declared on the `MonitorRunner` Protocol at `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/base.py:128-134`:

```python
supported_monitor_kinds: frozenset[str]

def run_monitors(
    self, *, table: str, schema: str | None, monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
) -> list[CheckOutcome]: ...
```

Return type is **`list[CheckOutcome]`**, one per `MonitorSpec`, in order (not a `SuiteOutcome`).

- `SnowflakeCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py:261-276`; delegates to `run_monitors_over_engine(self._engine.get(), table=table, schema=schema or self._config.schema_, catalog=None, monitors=monitors)`. Capability at `snowflake.py:179`: `supported_monitor_kinds: ClassVar[frozenset[str]] = frozenset({FRESHNESS, VOLUME})`.
- `UnityCatalogCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py:198-212`; same but `catalog=self._catalog` (3-part name). Capability at `unity_catalog.py:137`.
- Precedent for a **non-SQL** implementation: `IcebergCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py:336-360+`. It never builds SQL: loads the table once *before* the loop, then uses `run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, monitors=monitors, now=datetime.now(UTC))` with a native `_monitor_scalar`. **This is the exact template for a flat-file impl (#520)** — `run_monitor_specs` is the reusable seam.

## 2. `backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py` (282 lines)

- **`FlatFileCheckRunner`** — :121-157. Holds `conn_type`/`config`/`secret`. Only implements `run_checks` (:134-157) — **no `run_monitors`, no `supported_monitor_kinds`**. `table` carries the file path; `schema` unused (:126-127).
- **File read:** module-level `read_dataframe(*, conn_type, config, path, secret)` — :102-118 (note: public name, not `_read_dataframe`; the private `_read_dataframe` names are in `iceberg.py:302` and `profile_service.py:602`). Reads the **whole** file: `pd.read_csv(raw)` (:117) or `pd.read_parquet(raw, dtype_backend="pyarrow")` (:118). Format from `format_from_path` (:50-57, `.csv` / `.parquet|.pq` only).
  - **#476 delimiter assumption lives here at :117** — bare `pd.read_csv(raw)`, no `sep=`. Grep across `backend/app` confirms *every* CSV read hardcodes the pandas default comma: `flatfile.py:117`, `schema_drift.py:203`, `profile_service.py:638`, `profile_service.py:960`. No `sep=`/`delimiter=` anywhere in backend.
- **`list_file_columns` is NOT in this file** — it's `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/services/profile_service.py:934-969` (header-only: `pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=0).columns` at :960, or `pq.ParquetFile(raw).schema.names` at :963).
- **Batch resolution path** — :176-282:
  - `FileRef` dataclass :189-195 — `path: str`, `last_modified: datetime | None = None`.
  - `resolve_batch(files, *, pattern, strategy="latest", batch=None) -> str` :202-242 — pure; regex first capture group is the batch key; `latest` picks max key lexicographically (:241) or falls back to `_most_recent` (:199, `max` on `(last_modified or _MIN_DT, path)`); `specific` at :225-231.
  - `list_files(*, conn_type, config, prefix, secret) -> list[FileRef]` :245-267 — **the live seam, and this is where blob mtime IS available**:
    - **s3** (:249-256): `FileRef(path=obj["Key"], last_modified=obj.get("LastModified"))` — boto3 `list_objects_v2` paginator; mtime always present in practice.
    - **adls_gen2** (:258-267): `FileRef(path=blob.name, last_modified=getattr(blob, "last_modified", None))` — `container.list_blobs(name_starts_with=prefix)`.
    - **local**: there is **no local flat-file adapter**. `_FILE_TYPES = {"adls_gen2", "s3"}` (:44). Only `adls.py` and `s3.py` exist.
  - `resolve_batch_file(...)` :270-282 — list + resolve, called by `run_target.materialize_path` (`/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/services/run_target.py:150-186`, the `flatfile.resolve_batch_file` call at :178).
  - `download_bytes` :81-99 — object download; note **`download_bytes` does not return metadata**, so freshness-from-blob-mtime would need `list_files` (or a new head/properties call), not `download_bytes`.

## 3. Gating of monitor kinds

**What would be unified for #889:**
1. The **latest-run-per-suite DISTINCT ON** exists twice (`dashboard_service.py:205-212` vs `asset_view_service.py:269-274`).

**Router** — `checks.py:86` create, `:115` list, `:129` get, `:144` patch, `:173` delete, `:191` rebaseline, `:226/:243` snooze, `:285` versions, `:316` history, `:358` dryrun. All gated by `require_permission(db, suite_id, current_user.id, minimum="view"|"edit")`.

**Export/import round-trip** — `backend/app/services/suite_io_service.py`:
- `EXPORT_VERSION = 1` (`:48`) — "Bump when the document shape changes incompatibly"
- `export_suite:61-97` — the per-check doc dict is built at `:76-84` with exactly `name/kind/expectation_type/config/warn/fail/critical`. **Adding `dimension` requires touching this dict.**
- `import_suite:126-239` — validates all before writing; `Check(...)` construction at `:215-224`; `record_check_version` per check at `:229-230`
- Wire models: `backend/app/api/v1/suites.py` — `CheckDocument:283-294`, `SuiteDocument:297-304`, `export_suite:317`, `import_suite:332`

Also writes checks: `backend/app/mcp/server.py:350-397` (`create_check` MCP tool, `_reject_nul:95`).

## 3. `_roll_up` — `backend/app/services/asset_view_service.py:343-394`

Full body quoted in the file; the substance:
- Input: `list[RunOutcome]` for **all** composing suites (never grant-filtered).
- Accumulates `statuses` from each `run.worst_severity` (`:353-354`), then `worst_severity(statuses)` at `:385` — the shared `models.worst_severity` helper (rank-based, `critical > fail > warn`).
- `checks_total += run.checks_total; checks_passed += run.checks_passed` (`:371-372`) — **plain summation across suites**, no weighting, no per-dimension breakdown.
- `last_run_at` = max of `run.finished_at or run.created_at` (`:373-375`).
- Execution flags `:358-363`; connection-health flags `:367-370` (`failed or has_error → has_operational_error`; `has_skip`).

Upstream data path: `_latest_run_per_suite:265` (DISTINCT ON) → `_latest_outcomes:326-340` → `check_outcome_counts` + `operational_result_flags` (both in `run_service.py:385` / `:422`). `check_outcome_counts` returns `(total, passed, worst)` where total = `pass` + the three failing tiers, **excluding** `skip`/`error` (`run_service.py:412-418`).

**The one-rule authz/redaction pattern** (module docstring `:7-24`) — the ADR 0037 three-layer rule superseding #845/#846/#920:
- Identity & topology: workspace knowledge, never redacted.
- **Aggregate verdicts are workspace-true** — the rollup is computed over ALL composing suites for every viewer, explicitly citing "#889's two-verdicts-on-one-page problem" (`:16-17`). **A per-dimension scorecard must obey this: aggregate over all suites, not the caller's.**
- Itemized evaluation stays behind ADR 0027 suite grants; the rest collapse to `restricted_suite_count`.

Enforcement site: `get_visible_asset:431-483` — `effective_permissions:456` is the ONE visibility derivation (`:450-455` comment: "a suite is listed iff it has a label"); `summary = _roll_up(asset, [outcome_by_suite[s.id] for s in all_suites])` at `:464` uses `all_suites`, while `composing` uses `visible`. `list_visible_assets:400-428` takes no user at all.

## 4. `dashboard_service.py` — the ADR-0005 formula

- `_PENALTY:33-38` — `{pass:0.0, warn:0.5, fail:1.0, critical:2.0}`, `_W_MAX = 2.0` (`:39`)
- `_SEVERITY_STATUSES:43` — `tuple(_PENALTY)`, i.e. the four tiers; `skip`/`error` excluded from N
- `health_score:50-62` — `round(100.0 * (1.0 - penalty / (n * _W_MAX)), 1)`, `None` when n == 0
- `pass_rate:65-71`, `performance_state:74-82` (bands `_OPTIMAL_MIN=90.0`, `_STABLE_MIN=60.0` at `:46-47`)
- Parallel rollup math: `_status_counts:135-154` (histogram of `Result.status` joined to `Run`, windowed) and `_suite_performance:197-237` — its own DISTINCT ON latest-run subquery (`:205-212`) duplicating `asset_view_service._latest_run_per_suite:265`.

**What would be unified for #889:**
1. The **latest-run-per-suite DISTINCT ON** exists twice (`dashboard_service.py:205-212` vs `asset_view_service.py:269-274`).
2. `health_score`/`pass_rate` live only in `dashboard_service`, keyed off a `{status: count}` histogram — but `asset_view_service._roll_up` never builds a histogram, only `(total, passed, worst)`. A weighted asset score needs the full four-tier histogram, so either `check_outcome_counts` must return the histogram (it already computes `by_run[run_id][status]` internally at `run_service.py:407-409` and throws it away) or `_roll_up` must gain one.
3. `models.SEVERITY_RANK` (discrete order) vs `dashboard._PENALTY` (continuous weights) are deliberately separate concepts — `models.py:47-52` and `dashboard_service.py:28-30` both say so. `health_score` is the natural thing to lift into a shared module (it's pure, `Mapping[str,int] → float|None`); `_PENALTY` should NOT be merged into `SEVERITY_RANK`.
4. Scope difference to reconcile: dashboard is **grant-scoped** (`suite_service.accessible_suite_ids`, `:312`), asset view is **workspace-true**. A shared scorer must take the histogram as an argument and stay scope-agnostic.

## 5. `Run.asset_id` + `results` columns

- `Run.asset_id` — `models.py:589-591`, `ondelete="SET NULL"`, nullable, indexed (`ix_runs_asset_id`, `:560`). Docstring `:585-588`: "stamped at dispatch (ADR 0034)… never rewrites when a suite's target later changes."
- Stamping: `backend/app/services/run_dispatch.py:32-45` — `new_queued_run` sets `asset_id=suite.asset_id` (`:43`); the docstring warns `orchestration_service` builds its Run via `pg_insert` with "a matching inline `asset_id` subquery — keep the two in sync."
- `Result` — `models.py:607-637`: `run_id:616` (CASCADE, `ix_results_run_id`), `check_id:622` (CASCADE, `ix_results_check_id`), `status:625` (CHECK over `RESULT_STATUSES`), `metric_value:629` (Numeric), `duration_ms:630`, `observed_value/expected_value/sample_failures:633-635` (JSONB `none_as_null`), `created_at:637`.

Note for #889: a per-dimension aggregate needs `results ⋈ checks` on `check_id` (the dimension lives on the check, not the result). `ix_results_check_id` exists; `Run.asset_id` lets you go asset → runs directly without the suite hop.

## 6. Frontend

**Check editor** (where `expectation_type`/`kind` are chosen):
- `frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts` — the catalog. `CheckKind` type at `:27`; `ExpectationCategory:37` and `EXPECTATION_CATEGORIES:39-48` (`'Column values' | 'Table shape' | 'Freshness' | 'Volume' | 'Schema' | 'Custom SQL' | 'Comparison'`) — **an existing classification axis, orthogonal to DQ dimensions**; `ExpectationSpec:80-92`; `EXPECTATION_BY_TYPE` lookup at `:337`. This is the file ADR 0036 §4 says must become a backend catalog.
- `frontend/src/pages/CheckNew.tsx` — 3-step wizard: category → expectation → config; `spec` at `:54`; submit `:63-76`
- `frontend/src/pages/CheckEdit.tsx` — `expectation_type` Select at `:173-190`, prefill `:125-126`
- `frontend/src/components/checks/checkForm.ts` — `buildCheckPayload:63-76` (`kind: spec?.kind ?? 'expectation'`), `buildComparisonPayload:81-114`
- `frontend/src/components/checks/CheckHistoryDrawer.tsx:33` — version drawer
- Wire types: `frontend/src/api/suites.ts:121,150,162,216,271,308`

**Asset detail** — `frontend/src/pages/AssetDetail.tsx` (509 lines): `AssetDetailBody:93`; the two-axis header `HealthAxis` calls at `:128-137`; `HealthAxis` component `:212-225`; `DescriptionBlock:231`, `OwnerBlock:317`, `SuitesSection:414` (per-suite `checks_passed / checks_total` render at `:459`), then `IncidentsPanel`, `LineageGraph`, `ColumnLineagePanel`. The scorecard slots naturally between the header and `SuitesSection`.

**Assets list** — `frontend/src/pages/Assets.tsx`: `AssetsTable:166`, `columns:167-213` — Asset / Env / Suites (`suite_count`, `:191-196`) / Health (`AssetHealthTag`, `:197-202`) / Last seen. A new score column goes here.

**Health derivation** — `frontend/src/components/assets/health.ts`: `assetHealth:41-52`, `connectionHealth:74-94`, `suiteHealth:110-124`, `runHealth:127-134`. `suiteHealth` is the label-only analogue of what #889 wants to make numeric. Also consumed by `frontend/src/components/dashboard/AssetHealthLead.tsx:198`.

API client types: `frontend/src/api/assets.ts` (`AssetSummary`, `RunOutcome`).

## 7. Alembic

**Head revision: `a3b4c5d6e7f8`** — `backend/alembic/versions/a3b4c5d6e7f8_pipeline_runs_connection_fk_cascade.py` (34 revisions total, single head, verified by set difference of revision/down_revision).

Naming convention: `<12-hex-revision-id>_<snake_case_slug>.py`. Ids are hand-authored hex-looking strings (e.g. `d1e2f3a4b5c6`, `c4d5e6f7a8b9`), not `alembic revision` autogenerated hashes — pick one in that style. File header convention (`d1e2f3a4b5c6_connection_poll_health.py:1-40`): a prose docstring with `Revision ID:` / `Revises:` / `Create Date:` plus a rationale section, then `revision = "..."`, `down_revision = "..."`, `branch_labels = None`, `depends_on = None`. Newer files use bare `revision = ` while older use `revision: str = `; both present. Backward-compat rule stated inline at `:32-35`: "all three are nullable or carry a server default, so the currently-deployed code — which never writes them — keeps working against this schema… (two-step deploy)."

Precedent migrations to copy for a nullable-with-default enum column: `b7c8d9e0f1a2_add_check_source_connection.py`, `c4d5e6f7a8b9_add_check_versions.py`.

## 8. Existing tests

- `backend/tests/services/test_asset_view_service.py` (208 lines) — `test_summarize_asset_with_no_suites:44`, `test_error_result_feeds_connection_health_not_suite_health:136`, `test_skip_result_is_degraded_not_an_error:150`, `test_failed_run_is_an_operational_error:163`, `test_failing_data_does_not_touch_connection_health:174`, `test_cancelled_run_is_flagged_so_it_never_rolls_up_green:189`
- `backend/tests/services/test_dashboard_service.py` (336 lines) — parametrized `test_health_score:37`, `test_health_score_excludes_skip_and_error_from_n:41`, `test_pass_rate:56`, `test_performance_state:72`, plus scoping/window/delta tests `:130-336`
- `backend/tests/api/test_checks.py` (1444 lines) — the big CRUD + validation + version-history suite
- `backend/tests/api/test_assets.py` (789 lines) — asset endpoint + ADR 0037 workspace-true assertions
- `backend/tests/api/test_dashboard.py` (131), `backend/tests/api/test_suites.py` (export/import round-trip), `backend/tests/api/test_comparison_checks.py`, `backend/tests/services/test_run_service.py`, `backend/tests/services/test_severity.py`

---

**Two design constraints worth flagging up front.** First, ADR 0036 (`docs/adr/0036-connection-anchored-check-engines.md`, Accepted 2026-07-17) names #124 and #889 by number and asserts the dimension catalog and the engine-aware backend catalog are "one story… not two catalogs" — so #124 as scoped here may be a slice of a larger already-decided design rather than a standalone column. Second, `asset_view_service`'s module docstring cites "#889's two-verdicts-on-one-page problem" as the *reason* the rollup is workspace-true; a per-dimension scorecard that filtered by caller grants would reintroduce exactly the bug ADR 0037 was written to kill.

content.text:
Recon is in. Both groups are well-seamed, but three decisions materially change the build — let me get your call before I start.

**What I found that shapes the plan:**

- **#520 is small** — `run_monitor_specs` in [monitors.py:352](backend/app/datasources/monitors.py#L352) is already the datasource-agnostic seam, and `IcebergCheckRunner.run_monitors` ([iceberg.py:336](backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py#L336)) is a non-SQL template to copy. Blob `last_modified` is already plumbed through `list_files` → `FileRef`. Four touch points: the runner, `check_service.MONITOR_CAPABLE_TYPES:84`, `connections.ts:98`, `run_service.py:203`.
- **#476 is two unrelated bugs.** The CSV delimiter is hardcoded in **four** places (`flatfile.py:117`, `schema_drift.py:203`, `profile_service.py:638`, `:960`) — no `sep=` anywhere in the backend. The casing half is bigger than it reads: `is_sql_identifier` is a deliberate single-point seam, but widening it means `monitors.py:204/240` must gain real quoting, because unlike the profiler's SQLAlchemy Core builders it **f-string-interpolates raw names**.
- **#889 has a prerequisite that's real**: the latest-run-per-suite DISTINCT ON exists twice, and `check_outcome_counts` already computes a four-tier histogram internally ([run_service.py:407](backend/app/services/run_service.py#L407)) and throws it away — which is exactly what a weighted score needs.
- **One flag on #124**: ADR 0036 §4 names #124/#889 and asserts dimension classification is "one story" with turning the frontend expectation catalog into an engine-aware *backend* catalog. I do **not** think we should pull that rewrite in — I'd land `dimension` + a backend derivation map, which is the seed of that catalog, and let ADR 0036 consume it later. Say so if you disagree.

questions.question: #476's casing half asks us to 'decide whether v1 supports quoted/mixed-case identifiers'. Which way?

options.label: Fail loudly + document (Recommended)
options.description: Keep the narrow allowlist, but make the failure honest: introspection flags a column as unsupported instead of offering a name the profiler/monitor can't resolve, and docs state the limitation. Small, no quoting risk on the monitor SQL path.

options.label: Full quoted-identifier support
options.description: Widen is_sql_identifier and thread a dialect-correct quoting strategy through profile_service's Core builders AND monitors.py's f-string SQL + schema_drift. Bigger, and touches every SQL-emitting path at once — with no live Snowflake to verify mixed-case behaviour against.

options.label: Both, column optional (Recommended)
options.description: If the check config names a timestamp column, use MAX(col) over the resolved batch (mirrors SQL semantics); if not, fall back to the resolved file's blob last_modified. Catches 'no new file' AND 'stale rows in a fresh file'.

options.label: File last_modified only
options.description: Cheapest — no read of the file needed. Catches 'the source stopped producing' but misses stale in-file timestamps, and diverges from the SQL freshness semantics.

options.label: Column MAX only
options.description: Exact parity with the Snowflake/UC semantics. But a file that stops arriving entirely is invisible — arguably the more common flat-file incident.

options.label: Derived default, stored override (Recommended)
options.description: Backend expectation_type/kind → dimension map supplies a default at create time; the value is stored on the check and the author can override it in the editor. Matches the issue's AC, survives GX catalog drift, and gives #889 a plain column to GROUP BY.

options.label: Purely derived at read time
options.description: No stored column for the classification — compute from expectation_type on every read. No migration, but no user override, and #889's aggregation must join through a Python map instead of SQL GROUP BY.

options.label: Purely user-set
options.description: Nullable column, author picks it. Simplest code, but existing checks and most new ones land with no dimension, so the scorecard's coverage view is empty until someone backfills by hand.

content.text:
Decisions locked. Here's the plan — six PRs, one functionality each per CONTRIBUTING:

**Group 1 — flat-file**
1. **#476a — CSV delimiter.** Collapse the four hardcoded comma reads into one shared reader; delimiter sniffed from the header with an explicit connection/target override winning over the sniff. Applies to `read_dataframe`, `list_file_columns`, profiler `_read_dataframe`, `schema_drift`.
2. **#476b — quoted/mixed-case identifiers, end-to-end.** Widen `is_sql_identifier` (the documented single-point seam) and thread dialect-correct quoting through profile_service's Core builders **and** `monitors.py`/`schema_drift` — the f-string paths are the risky half and get the injection battery.
3. **#520 — flat-file freshness/volume.** `run_monitors` on `FlatFileCheckRunner` via `run_monitor_specs`; freshness = `MAX(col)` when the config names a column, else the resolved file's `last_modified`; volume = row count. Ungate `check_service:84` + `connections.ts:98`.

**Group 2 — dimension & score**
4. **#124 — `Check.dimension`.** ADR for the taxonomy (your canonical seven), additive nullable column + `CheckVersion` snapshot, backend `expectation_type`/`kind` → dimension derivation with user override at create *and* any time after, editor dropdown, export/import round-trip.
5. **#889a — rollup unification** (its own AC): one shared latest-run-per-suite query + one severity/status scorer used by `dashboard_service` and `asset_view_service`; `check_outcome_counts` stops discarding its four-tier histogram.
6. **#889b — the scorecard** on top: per-dimension coverage + weighted score, workspace-true per ADR 0037, asset page panel + list column.

Starting on #476a.

1	"""Flat-file (ADLS Gen2 / S3) IO + GX `CheckRunner`.
2	
3	Two responsibilities for the flat-file datasources, both behind the same
4	primitives the SQL adapters use (raw config dict + resolved secret, never the ORM
5	row — keeps `datasources/` decoupled from `db/`):
6	
7	* **IO** — `download_bytes` fetches an object/blob; `read_dataframe` parses it
8	  into pandas. Shared by the column profiler (service layer) and the runner.
9	* **Runner** — `FlatFileCheckRunner` runs GX expectations against the file by
10	  loading it into an in-memory pandas DataFrame and handing that to GX's pandas
11	  datasource, then mapping the result via the shared `gx_runner` machinery. The
12	  `CheckRunner` interface is table-shaped; for a flat-file datasource the
13	  ``table`` argument carries the **file path** and ``schema`` is unused.
14	
15	GX runs entirely in-process on the DataFrame, so — unlike the warehouse runners —
16	the run path is fully testable with a canned frame; only the network download is
17	the deferred-smoke seam.
18	"""
19	
20	from __future__ import annotations
21	
22	import io
23	import re
24	from dataclasses import dataclass
25	from datetime import UTC, datetime
26	from typing import Any
27	
28	import great_expectations as gx
29	
30	from backend.app.core.secrets import SecretStore
31	from backend.app.datasources.adls import AdlsConfig
32	from backend.app.datasources.base import CheckSpec, SuiteOutcome
33	from backend.app.datasources.gx_runner import run_expectations
34	from backend.app.datasources.s3 import S3Config
35	
36	# Connector timeouts (seconds): fail fast rather than hang the worker thread.
37	# _READ_TIMEOUT is deliberately longer than the SQL profiler's network timeout
38	# (profile_service._NETWORK_TIMEOUT = 30): it covers a full-object download (the
39	# whole CSV/Parquet is pulled before parsing), not a single warehouse query, so a
40	# large file legitimately needs more headroom. Not accidental drift (#147).
41	_CONNECT_TIMEOUT = 10
42	_READ_TIMEOUT = 60
43	
44	_FILE_TYPES = {"adls_gen2", "s3"}
45	
46	# Sort floor for files the store reports without a modified time.
47	_MIN_DT = datetime.min.replace(tzinfo=UTC)
48	
49	
50	def format_from_path(path: str) -> str | None:
51	    """Infer the file format from the path extension (`None` if unrecognised)."""
52	    lower = path.lower()
53	    if lower.endswith(".csv"):
54	        return "csv"
55	    if lower.endswith((".parquet", ".pq")):
56	        return "parquet"
57	    return None
58	
59	
60	def _s3_client(cfg: S3Config, secret: str) -> Any:
61	    """A boto3 S3 client for `cfg` with the standard fail-fast timeouts."""
62	    import boto3
63	    from botocore.config import Config
64	
65	    return boto3.client(
66	        "s3",
67	        region_name=cfg.region,
68	        aws_access_key_id=cfg.access_key_id,
69	        aws_secret_access_key=secret,
70	        config=Config(connect_timeout=_CONNECT_TIMEOUT, read_timeout=_READ_TIMEOUT),
71	    )
72	
73	
74	def _blob_service(acfg: AdlsConfig, secret: str) -> Any:
75	    """An ADLS `BlobServiceClient` for `acfg` (caller must `.close()` it)."""
76	    from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient
77	
78	    return BlobServiceClient(account_url=acfg.account_url, credential=secret)
79	
80	
81	def download_bytes(*, conn_type: str, config: dict[str, Any], path: str, secret: str) -> bytes:
82	    """Fetch the object/blob bytes from S3 or ADLS Gen2 (live seam).
83	
84	    Takes the connection's `type` + raw `config` + resolved `secret` (the caller
85	    owns the SecretStore), so this module never touches the DB.
86	    """
87	    if conn_type == "s3":
88	        cfg = S3Config.model_validate(config)
89	        body: bytes = _s3_client(cfg, secret).get_object(Bucket=cfg.bucket, Key=path)["Body"].read()
90	        return body
91	
92	    acfg = AdlsConfig.model_validate(config)
93	    client_az = _blob_service(acfg, secret)
94	    try:
95	        blob = client_az.get_blob_client(container=acfg.container, blob=path)
96	        downloaded: bytes = blob.download_blob().readall()
97	        return downloaded
98	    finally:
99	        client_az.close()
100	
101	
102	def read_dataframe(*, conn_type: str, config: dict[str, Any], path: str, secret: str) -> Any:
103	    """Download and parse the **whole** file into a pandas DataFrame (live seam).
104	
105	    The runner reads the full file — every row and column — because a check may
106	    reference any column and row-count/null checks must be exact (unlike the
107	    profiler, which samples + projects). Raises `ValueError` for an unknown
108	    format.
109	    """
110	    import pandas as pd
111	
112	    fmt = format_from_path(path)
113	    if fmt is None:
114	        raise ValueError(f"unsupported flat-file format for path {path!r}")

"""Flat-file (ADLS Gen2 / S3) IO + GX `CheckRunner`.

Two responsibilities for the flat-file datasources, both behind the same
primitives the SQL adapters use (raw config dict + resolved secret, never the ORM
row — keeps `datasources/` decoupled from `db/`):

* **IO** — `download_bytes` fetches an object/blob; `read_dataframe` parses it
  into pandas. Shared by the column profiler (service layer) and the runner.
* **Runner** — `FlatFileCheckRunner` runs GX expectations against the file by
  loading it into an in-memory pandas DataFrame and handing that to GX's pandas
  datasource, then mapping the result via the shared `gx_runner` machinery. The
  `CheckRunner` interface is table-shaped; for a flat-file datasource the
  ``table`` argument carries the **file path** and ``schema`` is unused.

GX runs entirely in-process on the DataFrame, so — unlike the warehouse runners —
the run path is fully testable with a canned frame; only the network download is
the deferred-smoke seam.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import io
import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import UTC, datetime
from typing import Any

import great_expectations as gx

from backend.app.core.secrets import SecretStore
from backend.app.datasources.adls import AdlsConfig
from backend.app.datasources.base import CheckSpec, SuiteOutcome
from backend.app.datasources.gx_runner import run_expectations
from backend.app.datasources.s3 import S3Config

# Connector timeouts (seconds): fail fast rather than hang the worker thread.
# _READ_TIMEOUT is deliberately longer than the SQL profiler's network timeout
# (profile_service._NETWORK_TIMEOUT = 30): it covers a full-object download (the
# whole CSV/Parquet is pulled before parsing), not a single warehouse query, so a
# large file legitimately needs more headroom. Not accidental drift (#147).
_CONNECT_TIMEOUT = 10
_READ_TIMEOUT = 60

_FILE_TYPES = {"adls_gen2", "s3"}

# Sort floor for files the store reports without a modified time.
_MIN_DT = datetime.min.replace(tzinfo=UTC)


def format_from_path(path: str) -> str | None:
    """Infer the file format from the path extension (`None` if unrecognised)."""
    lower = path.lower()
    if lower.endswith(".csv"):
        return "csv"
    if lower.endswith((".parquet", ".pq")):
        return "parquet"
    return None


def _s3_client(cfg: S3Config, secret: str) -> Any:
    """A boto3 S3 client for `cfg` with the standard fail-fast timeouts."""
    import boto3
    from botocore.config import Config

    return boto3.client(
        "s3",
        region_name=cfg.region,
        aws_access_key_id=cfg.access_key_id,
        aws_secret_access_key=secret,
        config=Config(connect_timeout=_CONNECT_TIMEOUT, read_timeout=_READ_TIMEOUT),
    )


def _blob_service(acfg: AdlsConfig, secret: str) -> Any:
    """An ADLS `BlobServiceClient` for `acfg` (caller must `.close()` it)."""
    from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

    return BlobServiceClient(account_url=acfg.account_url, credential=secret)


def download_bytes(*, conn_type: str, config: dict[str, Any], path: str, secret: str) -> bytes:
    """Fetch the object/blob bytes from S3 or ADLS Gen2 (live seam).

    Takes the connection's `type` + raw `config` + resolved `secret` (the caller
    owns the SecretStore), so this module never touches the DB.
    """
    if conn_type == "s3":
        cfg = S3Config.model_validate(config)
        body: bytes = _s3_client(cfg, secret).get_object(Bucket=cfg.bucket, Key=path)["Body"].read()
        return body

    acfg = AdlsConfig.model_validate(config)
    client_az = _blob_service(acfg, secret)
    try:
        blob = client_az.get_blob_client(container=acfg.container, blob=path)
        downloaded: bytes = blob.download_blob().readall()
        return downloaded
    finally:
        client_az.close()


def read_dataframe(*, conn_type: str, config: dict[str, Any], path: str, secret: str) -> Any:
    """Download and parse the **whole** file into a pandas DataFrame (live seam).

    The runner reads the full file — every row and column — because a check may
    reference any column and row-count/null checks must be exact (unlike the
    profiler, which samples + projects). Raises `ValueError` for an unknown
    format.
    """
    import pandas as pd

    fmt = format_from_path(path)
    if fmt is None:
        raise ValueError(f"unsupported flat-file format for path {path!r}")
    raw = io.BytesIO(download_bytes(conn_type=conn_type, config=config, path=path, secret=secret))
    if fmt == "csv":
        return pd.read_csv(raw)
    return pd.read_parquet(raw, dtype_backend="pyarrow")

    """`CheckRunner` for flat files — loads the file into pandas, runs GX on it.

    Holds the resolved credential (like `SnowflakeCheckRunner` holds its
    connection string), so `run_checks` is self-contained. ``table`` is the file
    path; ``schema`` is ignored (flat files have no schema namespace).
    """

    def __init__(self, *, conn_type: str, config: dict[str, Any], secret: str) -> None:
        self._conn_type = conn_type

def _read_dataframe(
    connection: Connection,
    *,
    path: str,
    file_format: str,
    columns: list[str],
    secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> Any:
    """Download `path` from the flat-file datasource into a sampled dataframe.

    The live I/O seam (download + parse) — monkeypatched in tests. Applies the two
    "load less data" levers from the pandas scaling guide:

    * **column projection** — only the requested `columns` are parsed (CSV
      `usecols`, Parquet `columns=`), so profiling 3 of 200 columns doesn't read
      all 200. Unknown names are simply not selected; `profile_dataframe` then
      reports genuinely-missing ones as a clean 422.
    * **row sampling** — at most `_SAMPLE_ROWS` rows (CSV pushes the cap into the
      parser; Parquet is sliced after the projected read).

    Not done (deliberate, for an authoring-time sampler): streaming/range reads —
    the whole object is still downloaded before parsing — and out-of-core engines
    (Dask). Both are future work if a profiling-cost problem actually shows up.
    """
    import pandas as pd

    if not connection.secret_ref:
        raise ValueError("connection requires secret_ref for the credential")
    secret = secret_store.get(connection.secret_ref)
    wanted = set(columns)
    raw = io.BytesIO(
        download_bytes(
            conn_type=connection.type, config=connection.config, path=path, secret=secret
        )
    )
    if file_format == "csv":
        return pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=_SAMPLE_ROWS, usecols=lambda name: name in wanted)

    import pyarrow.parquet as pq

    available = set(pq.ParquetFile(raw).schema.names)
    raw.seek(0)
    present = [c for c in columns if c in available]
    # Parquet is already Arrow on disk; dtype_backend="pyarrow" keeps the buffers
    # zero-copy instead of materialising a numpy copy. The stat helpers + the
    # _to_native coercion are Arrow-scalar-safe (min/max → Python int/str,
    # timestamps → Timestamp.isoformat, NA dropped before reductions).
    return pd.read_parquet(raw, columns=present, dtype_backend="pyarrow").head(_SAMPLE_ROWS)

"""AWS S3 connection adapter.

A flat-file datasource (CLAUDE.md §4): DQ checks run against files in a bucket.
Week 2 ships only the `ConnectionAdapter` seam (config validation + connectivity
`test`); the GX pandas/S3 `CheckRunner` that reads the files is a Week-3 concern.

**Auth — v1 supports static access keys only.** The roadmap lists IAM role *and*
access key, but an IAM role has no stored secret and is only meaningfully
testable once DataQ runs on AWS with an instance/task role — so it (and the
broader ``connections.secret_ref`` nullability change) is deferred to Week 7. An
``iam_role`` config is accepted by the schema but rejected with a clear message
in v1; use an access key (``access_key_id`` in config, secret access key in the
SecretStore).

``test`` issues ``head_bucket`` — a green test means the credentials authenticate
and the bucket is reachable. ``boto3`` is imported lazily (per ``core/secrets.py``)
so non-S3 deployments don't pay the import cost; retries are disabled so an
auth/endpoint failure surfaces immediately. Like the other adapters it runs live
and fails-soft pending real credentials.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any, Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, model_validator

# Fail fast rather than hang the request thread on an unreachable endpoint.
_TEST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 10


class S3Config(BaseModel):
    """Non-secret S3 connection config (the secret access key comes from secrets).

    Maps from ``Connection.config``. ``access_key_id`` is the non-secret half of
    the static credential; the secret access key is resolved from the SecretStore
    at test time.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    bucket: str
    region: str
    auth_type: Literal["access_key", "iam_role"] = "access_key"
    access_key_id: str | None = None

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _check_auth(self) -> S3Config:
        if self.auth_type == "iam_role":
            raise ValueError(
                "iam_role auth is deferred to Week 7 (needs an ambient AWS role to "
                "test against); use auth_type='access_key' with an access key in v1"
            )
        if not self.access_key_id:
            raise ValueError("access_key_id is required when auth_type is 'access_key'")
        return self


class S3ConnectionAdapter:
    """`ConnectionAdapter` for AWS S3 — config validation + a head_bucket probe."""
===== ADLS =====
"""ADLS Gen2 (Azure Data Lake Storage) connection adapter.

A flat-file datasource (CLAUDE.md §4): DQ checks run against files in a
container. Week 2 ships only the `ConnectionAdapter` seam (config validation +
connectivity `test`); the GX pandas/ABFS `CheckRunner` that reads the files is a
Week-3 concern.

**Auth — v1 supports SAS only.** The roadmap lists managed-identity *and* SAS,
but managed identity has no stored secret and is only meaningfully testable once
DataQ runs on Azure with an ambient identity — so it (and the broader
``connections.secret_ref`` nullability change) is deferred to Week 7. A
``managed_identity`` config is accepted by the schema but rejected with a clear
message in v1; use a SAS token (held in the SecretStore).

``test`` builds a ``BlobServiceClient`` from the account URL + SAS and reads the
container's properties — a green test means the endpoint is reachable, the SAS
authenticates, and the container exists. The Azure SDK is imported lazily (per
``core/secrets.py``) so non-ADLS deployments don't pay the import cost; like the
other adapters it runs live and fails-soft pending real credentials.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any, Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, field_validator, model_validator

# Fail fast rather than hang the request thread on an unreachable account.
_TEST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 10


class AdlsConfig(BaseModel):
    """Non-secret ADLS Gen2 connection config (the SAS token comes from secrets).

    Maps from ``Connection.config``. ``account_url`` is the storage endpoint
    (e.g. ``https://<account>.blob.core.windows.net``); ``container`` is the
    filesystem. The SAS token is resolved from the SecretStore at test time.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    account_url: str
    container: str
    auth_type: Literal["sas", "managed_identity"] = "sas"

    @field_validator("account_url")
    @classmethod
    def _http_url(cls, value: str) -> str:
        if not value.startswith(("http://", "https://")):
            raise ValueError("account_url must start with http:// or https://")
        return value.rstrip("/")

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _managed_identity_deferred(self) -> AdlsConfig:
        if self.auth_type == "managed_identity":
            raise ValueError(
                "managed_identity auth is deferred to Week 7 (needs an ambient Azure "
                "identity to test against); use auth_type='sas' with a SAS token in v1"
            )
        return self

content.text: Design call on the delimiter half: I'm going with **per-file sniffing, no config field**. A connection is a whole bucket/container — different files under it legitimately use different delimiters, so a per-connection hint is the wrong granularity, and sniffing needs no migration, no frontend spec change, and no user action. Candidate delimiters are restricted to `,;\t|` with a comma fallback so a failed sniff degrades to today's behaviour.

_FILE_TYPES = {"adls_gen2", "s3"}

# Sort floor for files the store reports without a modified time.
_MIN_DT = datetime.min.replace(tzinfo=UTC)

# Delimiters `sniff_delimiter` will consider, and the fallback when it can't tell
# (which is also the pre-#476 behaviour, so a failed sniff never regresses a file
# that parses today). Deliberately a short allowlist rather than letting
# `csv.Sniffer` pick freely: given a header like `name,title` it will happily
# nominate `e` or a space as the delimiter, which is a *worse* silent wrong
# answer than assuming a comma.
_CSV_DELIMITERS = ",;\t|"
_DEFAULT_DELIMITER = ","

# How much of the object to hand the sniffer. The header plus a few rows is
# plenty and keeps the decode bounded on a large file.
_SNIFF_BYTES = 64 * 1024


def format_from_path(path: str) -> str | None:
    """Infer the file format from the path extension (`None` if unrecognised)."""
    lower = path.lower()
    if lower.endswith(".csv"):
        return "csv"
    if lower.endswith((".parquet", ".pq")):
        return "parquet"
    return None


def sniff_delimiter(sample: bytes) -> str:
    """Guess a CSV's delimiter from its leading bytes, falling back to a comma.

    Pure (no IO) so the decision is testable without a datasource. Sniffing is
    per-*file* on purpose: a flat-file connection is a whole bucket/container and
    the files under it need not agree on a delimiter, so a per-connection hint
    would be the wrong granularity.

    Never raises — an undecidable sample (single column, empty file, binary junk)
    yields `_DEFAULT_DELIMITER`.
    """
    text = sample.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
    # Sniff over whole lines only: a sample cut mid-row can end in a fragment
    # whose field counts don't line up, which is exactly what Sniffer keys off.
    head, newline, _ = text.rpartition("\n")
    if newline:
        text = head
    if not text.strip():
        return _DEFAULT_DELIMITER
    try:
        return csv.Sniffer().sniff(text, delimiters=_CSV_DELIMITERS).delimiter
    except csv.Error:
        return _DEFAULT_DELIMITER


def read_csv_bytes(raw: io.BytesIO, **kwargs: Any) -> Any:
    """`pd.read_csv` over `raw` with the delimiter sniffed from its header (#476).

    The single CSV-parsing seam for every flat-file path — runner, profiler,
    column lister, schema-drift introspection — so they can never disagree about
    what a file's columns are. Extra `kwargs` (``nrows``, ``usecols``, …) pass
    straight through; the buffer is rewound before parsing, so callers may hand
    over a buffer at any position.
    """
    import pandas as pd

    sep = sniff_delimiter(raw.getvalue()[:_SNIFF_BYTES])
    raw.seek(0)
    return pd.read_csv(raw, sep=sep, **kwargs)

@pytest.mark.parametrize(

    [
        (b"a,b,c\n1,2,3\n4,5,6\n", ","),
        (b"a;b;c\n1;2;3\n4;5;6\n", ";"),
        (b"a\tb\tc\n1\t2\t3\n4\t5\t6\n", "\t"),
        (b"a|b|c\n1|2|3\n4|5|6\n", "|"),
    ],
)
def test_sniff_delimiter_detects_each_supported_delimiter(sample: bytes, expected: str) -> None:
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(sample) == expected


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "sample",
    [
        b"",  # empty file
        b"\n\n  \n",  # whitespace only
        b"only_one_column\n1\n2\n",  # nothing to infer a delimiter from
        b"\x00\x01\x02\xff\xfe",  # binary junk (undecodable)
        b"a,b\n",  # header with no data rows
    ],
)
def test_sniff_delimiter_falls_back_to_comma_when_undecidable(sample: bytes) -> None:
    """An unsniffable sample must degrade to the pre-#476 behaviour, never raise —
    a wrong-but-unchanged answer beats a 502 on a file that parses today."""
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(sample) == ","


def test_sniff_delimiter_ignores_a_truncated_trailing_row() -> None:
    """The sniff sample is a byte prefix, so the last line is usually cut. A
    fragment with mismatched field counts is what Sniffer keys off, so it must be
    dropped — otherwise the delimiter flips depending on where the cut landed."""
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(b"a;b;c\n1;2;3\n4;5") == ";"


def test_sniff_delimiter_never_picks_a_delimiter_outside_the_allowlist() -> None:
    """Left free, csv.Sniffer nominates letters/spaces on prose-ish headers. The
    allowlist keeps a bad guess bounded to a comma."""
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(b"name title\nalice engineer\nbob analyst\n") == ","

The **Column values are of type** expectation's `type_` field is the one place the
check editor's "obvious" answer is usually wrong. GX validates it against a
*different* type vocabulary depending on which engine actually runs the check — not
the type your warehouse/catalog shows you:

- **Snowflake** builds a real SQL batch (`SqlAlchemyExecutionEngine`) and string-compares
  `type_` against the **fully-qualified dialect type**, not the short column type. A
  `NUMBER` column reports as `DECIMAL(38, 0)`; `VARCHAR` reports as `VARCHAR(16777216)`.
  Plugging in `NUMBER` or `DECIMAL` alone fails every time.
- **Unity Catalog, ADLS Gen2 / S3, and Apache Iceberg** all read the target into a
  pandas DataFrame first (`PandasExecutionEngine`). GX first tries an **exact dtype
  match**; only when the column's dtype is `object` and `type_` isn't
  `object`/`object_`/`O` does it fall back to a **row-wise Python value-type compare**.
  In practice:
  - Numeric columns report numpy dtypes — `int64`, `float64`, `bool`. **Caveat:** an
    integer column containing *any* NULL is upcast to `float64` by the read
    (`pd.read_sql_table` / `pd.read_csv`), so a nullable `BIGINT` reports `float64`,
    not `int64`.
  - String columns on **Unity Catalog and CSV** reads are plain pandas `object` dtype
    (these reads are *not* Arrow-backed). Both `type_: object` (exact dtype match) and
    `type_: str` (row-wise value-type match) pass — pick either.
  - **Parquet and Iceberg** reads *are* Arrow-backed and can report Arrow-flavored
    dtype names — calibrate from a dry-run rather than assuming the CSV/UC names.

| Datasource | Engine | `type_` guidance |
|---|---|---|
| Snowflake | SQL (dialect-native) | `DECIMAL(38, 0)` for `NUMBER`, `VARCHAR(16777216)` for `VARCHAR` |
| Unity Catalog | pandas DataFrame (not Arrow-backed) | `int64` for non-nullable `BIGINT` (**`float64` if the column contains NULLs**); `object` or `str` for `STRING` |
| ADLS Gen2 / S3 (CSV) | pandas DataFrame (not Arrow-backed) | `int64`/`float64`/`bool` for numerics (**NULLs upcast integers to `float64`**); `object` or `str` for strings |
| ADLS Gen2 / S3 (Parquet) / Iceberg | pandas DataFrame (Arrow-backed) | Arrow-flavored dtype names — confirm via a dry-run's `observed_value` |

**Calibration tip:** don't guess — **dry-run first**, but know where the trail runs
out. On **Snowflake and the Arrow-backed sources** (Parquet/Iceberg), a failing
result's `observed_value` carries the *exact* string GX expected — copy it into
`type_` and re-run to confirm green. On **Unity Catalog / CSV**, a wrong value-type
guess (e.g. `int64` against a string column) falls to GX's row-wise compare, which
fails with **no observed value at all** — the dry-run preview renders Observed as
"—". If you see that, don't hunt for a magic string: the column is `object` dtype, so
enter `object` or the Python value type name (`str`). The check editor's help text
under the field repeats this per the suite's connection type.

Before saving any of them: **Dry-run** previews pass/fail against live data, and the
**column profiler** (nulls, distinct count, min/max, top values) helps place thresholds.

# Datasources & checks

## Supported datasources

| Datasource | Connection | Check authoring | Execution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Snowflake (DEV/QA/UAT) | account + user + key/PAT | ✅ | ✅ |
| ADLS Gen2 (flat files) | account URL + container, SAS | ✅ | ✅ |
| AWS S3 (flat files) | bucket + region, access key | ✅ | ✅ |
| Unity Catalog (Databricks) | workspace URL + warehouse + PAT | ✅ | ✅ |
| Apache Iceberg | catalog URI + catalog type (REST/SQL/Glue/Hive) + optional storage credential | ✅ | ✅ |

## Add a connection

In the UI, **Connections → Add connection**, pick the datasource, fill the type-specific
fields, and **Test** it (a live reachability probe). Credentials are stored in the secret
store (Azure Key Vault in production), never in the database.

Snowflake supports two auth modes: **password** and **key pair (RSA)**. For key pair,
paste the PEM private key; if the key is passphrase-protected (PKCS#8), fill the optional
**Key passphrase** field — both parts are stored together as one secret and rotate
atomically via **Re-auth**. Leave the passphrase blank for an unencrypted key.
Key-pair connections also require **Role** (the GX key-pair form mandates one for suite
runs, so it is validated when the connection is saved).

## Author a check

1. Create (or open) a **suite** and point it at a **target** — a table (Snowflake/UC), a
   file/path or batch pattern (ADLS/S3), or an Iceberg `namespace.table`.
2. **Add check** opens a dedicated page (`/suites/<id>/checks/new`): pick a **category**,
   then the check type, then fill its config. The four authoring paths:

### Flat files: formats and CSV delimiters

Flat-file connections (ADLS Gen2 / S3) read `.csv` and `.parquet`/`.pq`. **The CSV
delimiter is detected per file, not per connection** — a connection is a whole
bucket/container and the files under it need not agree, so DataQ sniffs each file's
header. Comma, semicolon, tab, and pipe are recognised; anything it can't decide
(a single-column file, an empty file) is read as comma-separated.

If a file uses some other separator, DataQ will parse the whole header as one
column — the symptom is a **column dropdown offering a single long name** like
`id;email;amount`. Convert the file to one of the four separators, or to Parquet.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Datasources & checks

## Supported datasources

| Datasource | Connection | Check authoring | Execution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Snowflake (DEV/QA/UAT) | account + user + key/PAT | ✅ | ✅ |
| ADLS Gen2 (flat files) | account URL + container, SAS | ✅ | ✅ |
| AWS S3 (flat files) | bucket + region, access key | ✅ | ✅ |
| Unity Catalog (Databricks) | workspace URL + warehouse + PAT | ✅ | ✅ |
| Apache Iceberg | catalog URI + catalog type (REST/SQL/Glue/Hive) + optional storage credential | ✅ | ✅ |

## Add a connection

In the UI, **Connections → Add connection**, pick the datasource, fill the type-specific
fields, and **Test** it (a live reachability probe). Credentials are stored in the secret
store (Azure Key Vault in production), never in the database.

Snowflake supports two auth modes: **password** and **key pair (RSA)**. For key pair,
paste the PEM private key; if the key is passphrase-protected (PKCS#8), fill the optional
**Key passphrase** field — both parts are stored together as one secret and rotate
atomically via **Re-auth**. Leave the passphrase blank for an unencrypted key.
Key-pair connections also require **Role** (the GX key-pair form mandates one for suite
runs, so it is validated when the connection is saved).

## Author a check

1. Create (or open) a **suite** and point it at a **target** — a table (Snowflake/UC), a
   file/path or batch pattern (ADLS/S3), or an Iceberg `namespace.table`.
2. **Add check** opens a dedicated page (`/suites/<id>/checks/new`): pick a **category**,
   then the check type, then fill its config. The four authoring paths:

### GX expectation (all datasources)

Pick a *Column values* / *Table shape* expectation (e.g. `Column values not null`), name
it, set the column, and optionally band severity with **Warn ≥ / Fail ≥ / Critical ≥**
thresholds over the unexpected-%. Leave thresholds blank for binary pass/fail.

### Custom SQL (Snowflake / Unity Catalog — ADR 0019)

A read-only SQL rule in the Monaco editor: **any rows returned are failures**. Use
`{batch}` as a placeholder for the suite's target table
(`SELECT * FROM {batch} WHERE amount < 0`). Single read-only statement enforced
server-side.

### Freshness monitor (SQL datasources + Iceberg — ADR 0012/0030)

*How stale is the table?* Point it at the load/updated **timestamp column**; the check
measures hours since `MAX(column)` and bands that age with the thresholds. A **fail or
critical threshold is required** — without one, a freshness check could never fail.

### Volume monitor (SQL datasources + Iceberg — ADR 0012/0030)

*Did the load deliver?* Set the expected **min/max row count**; thresholds optionally
band the % by which the count falls outside the range (a spike can exceed 100%), or
leave them blank for binary in-range pass/fail.

### Type names for `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type`

The **Column values are of type** expectation's `type_` field is the one place the
check editor's "obvious" answer is usually wrong. GX validates it against a
*different* type vocabulary depending on which engine actually runs the check — not
the type your warehouse/catalog shows you:

- **Snowflake** builds a real SQL batch (`SqlAlchemyExecutionEngine`) and string-compares
  `type_` against the **fully-qualified dialect type**, not the short column type. A
  `NUMBER` column reports as `DECIMAL(38, 0)`; `VARCHAR` reports as `VARCHAR(16777216)`.
  Plugging in `NUMBER` or `DECIMAL` alone fails every time.
- **Unity Catalog, ADLS Gen2 / S3, and Apache Iceberg** all read the target into a
  pandas DataFrame first (`PandasExecutionEngine`). GX first tries an **exact dtype
  match**; only when the column's dtype is `object` and `type_` isn't
  `object`/`object_`/`O` does it fall back to a **row-wise Python value-type compare**.
  In practice:
  - Numeric columns report numpy dtypes — `int64`, `float64`, `bool`. **Caveat:** an
    integer column containing *any* NULL is upcast to `float64` by the read
    (`pd.read_sql_table` / `pd.read_csv`), so a nullable `BIGINT` reports `float64`,
    not `int64`.
  - String columns on **Unity Catalog and CSV** reads are plain pandas `object` dtype
    (these reads are *not* Arrow-backed). Both `type_: object` (exact dtype match) and
    `type_: str` (row-wise value-type match) pass — pick either.
  - **Parquet and Iceberg** reads *are* Arrow-backed and can report Arrow-flavored
    dtype names — calibrate from a dry-run rather than assuming the CSV/UC names.

| Datasource | Engine | `type_` guidance |
|---|---|---|
| Snowflake | SQL (dialect-native) | `DECIMAL(38, 0)` for `NUMBER`, `VARCHAR(16777216)` for `VARCHAR` |
| Unity Catalog | pandas DataFrame (not Arrow-backed) | `int64` for non-nullable `BIGINT` (**`float64` if the column contains NULLs**); `object` or `str` for `STRING` |
| ADLS Gen2 / S3 (CSV) | pandas DataFrame (not Arrow-backed) | `int64`/`float64`/`bool` for numerics (**NULLs upcast integers to `float64`**); `object` or `str` for strings |
| ADLS Gen2 / S3 (Parquet) / Iceberg | pandas DataFrame (Arrow-backed) | Arrow-flavored dtype names — confirm via a dry-run's `observed_value` |

**Calibration tip:** don't guess — **dry-run first**, but know where the trail runs
out. On **Snowflake and the Arrow-backed sources** (Parquet/Iceberg), a failing
result's `observed_value` carries the *exact* string GX expected — copy it into
`type_` and re-run to confirm green. On **Unity Catalog / CSV**, a wrong value-type
guess (e.g. `int64` against a string column) falls to GX's row-wise compare, which
fails with **no observed value at all** — the dry-run preview renders Observed as
"—". If you see that, don't hunt for a magic string: the column is `object` dtype, so
enter `object` or the Python value type name (`str`). The check editor's help text
under the field repeats this per the suite's connection type.

Before saving any of them: **Dry-run** previews pass/fail against live data, and the
**column profiler** (nulls, distinct count, min/max, top values) helps place thresholds.

## Run it

Run a suite **now**, on a **[cron schedule](scheduling.md)**, or **triggered** by a
pipeline (see **[Orchestration](orchestration.md)**). Results land on the **Results**
page and the **Dashboard** (health score + trends); failures alert per the suite's
**[notification config](notifications.md)**.

Severity comes from thresholds banding the observed unexpected-percentage
(warn < fail < critical); see ADR 0005 / 0016 for the model, and
**[Best practices](best-practices.md)** for how to pick the bands.

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
fix(flatfile): sniff the CSV delimiter per file instead of assuming a comma

Every CSV read in the backend hardcoded the pandas default comma — the runner
(`read_dataframe`), the profiler sample (`_read_dataframe`), the column lister
(`list_file_columns`), and schema-drift introspection. A `;`/tab/pipe-delimited
file therefore parsed as ONE column whose name is the entire header line, with
no error raised: a silent wrong answer that defeats the column dropdown, makes
every check on that file unauthorable, and would report every real column as
simultaneously added and removed the first time drift ran.

Collapses the four sites onto one seam, `flatfile.read_csv_bytes`, which sniffs
the delimiter from the file's header before parsing. Sniffing is per-FILE on
purpose: a flat-file connection is a whole bucket/container and its files need
not share a delimiter, so a per-connection config hint would be the wrong
granularity (and would need a migration + a frontend spec field to express).

`csv.Sniffer` is constrained to an allowlist of `,;\t|` — left free it will
nominate a letter or a space as the delimiter for a prose-ish header, which is a
worse silent wrong answer than assuming a comma. An undecidable sample falls
back to comma, i.e. exactly today's behaviour, so no file that parses now can
regress. The sample is cut to whole lines: a byte-prefix ends mid-row, and a
fragment's mismatched field count is precisely what the sniffer keys off.

Part of #476 (the identifier-casing half follows separately).
EOF
